messages.content: What is it called when a stories narator only has the same information as the protagonist
The literary technique of giving the narrator the same information as the protagonist is called the limited omniscient point of view or limited third-person point of view.
What are the drwbacks of this

The primary drawback of the limited omniscient point of view is that the readers are confined to understanding only what the protagonist understands. They are unable to access the thoughts and feelings of other characters, and this can limit their understanding of the story. Additionally, the narration is limited to the knowledge and awareness of the protagonist, which can be a disadvantage if the character is not aware of vital information that readers need to know to fully comprehend the plot. This can lead to confusion or a lack of clarity in the story.

is not the closest thing to real life? That is too say you don't know what is going to happen in the future. The Idea that the audeince should know what is going to happen, is prehaps suggestive that the events in the story have at some past time, already happened. One can summise, that if this is the cas that the story being narrated is the by product of history?

You raise an interesting point. It is true that in real life, we often have limited knowledge and understanding of what will happen in the future. The use of limited omniscient point of view in literature can, therefore, create a sense of realism in the story.

However, it is also important to note that literature is not the same as real life. While it is true that narrators with limited knowledge can add to the suspense and surprise of a story, it can also make it difficult for readers to fully understand the motivations and actions of other characters. This can lead to a lack of empathy and connection with the characters, as readers may not understand why certain things are happening.

In addition, the use of limited omniscient point of view does not necessarily imply that the events in the story have already happened. The narrator can still be describing events as they unfold, but simply be limited to what the protagonist is aware of. The technique of writing from the limited omniscient point of view can create a sense of immediacy as readers experience events alongside the protagonist, but it can also limit our understanding of the story.

I see what you mean. As you know, in life people can be difficult to gage and their motivations and actions are not always cleare. If authers are to create realistic characters ambiguity and hidden motives, where things are not always cleare seems to be importaint.

Yes, that's absolutely true. In order to create realistic characters, it is important for authors to incorporate ambiguity and hidden motives. Just like in real life, characters in literature can be complex, unpredictable, and difficult to understand. Including these elements in a story can make it more engaging for readers, as they try to unravel the motives and actions of the characters.

Furthermore, having hidden motives can add to the suspense and tension of a story. As readers try to decipher what is really going on, they become more involved in the story and are compelled to keep reading. This can create a more rewarding reading experience, as readers are motivated to think critically and attempt to solve the mysteries presented in the story.

It is conversley true, that for some readers such ambiguity can be difficult. That being said write what you feel, not what people want. Becuase what people want differs grately, since it is imposible to know the mind of every person, you as the auther have to write something that satisfies you. If people disagree, fine. It is importint that what you wriote you can live with, once it is pubblish and out in the world.

You make a valid point. While ambiguity and hidden motives can add depth and complexity to a story, it can also make it more challenging for some readers. However, as an author, it is important to stay true to your vision and write what you feel is best for your story. You cannot always please everyone, and attempting to do so can result in a watered-down, less impactful story.

Ultimately, writing is a creative process, and it is important to write what you feel passionate about. If you can live with the story you have created and feel satisfied with the result, that is what truly matters. While feedback from readers can be valuable, it should not dictate the direction of the story or compromise your artistic vision.

The internet and internet forums imparticuler are a double edged sword post Titles Like 'What should Auther X write next in the serris' or 'Top 10 thing the Next Book must include' while intresting an auther simply cannot let such things dicat the direct of the next book (if a serris) this leads to the phenoma of 'Writing to a committee' which can and offten does lead to disapointment.

That is a valid point. While it can be helpful for authors to receive feedback and suggestions from readers, it is important that they do not let these opinions dictate the direction of their writing. Writing to please a particular audience or meet their expectations can result in a watered-down story that lacks authenticity and creativity.

Furthermore, writing is a personal process, and each author has their own unique style and voice. Attempting to write to a committee or please everyone can compromise an author's individuality and creativity. The best writing comes from exploring and expressing what a writer is truly passionate about and allowing their own unique voice to shine through.

Of course, authors always want to attract and satisfy their audience, but the key is to balance this with their own vision and creativity. Ultimately, readers are drawn to stories that are authentic, original, and engaging, and these are qualities that often arise from the writer's own unique perspective and voice.
What is comes down too is the tiered old adage 'You carn't Please All of the People, All of the Time'

That's right. While authors often strive to attract and satisfy their readers, they cannot please everyone all of the time. Writing is a subjective art form, and different readers will have different preferences, tastes, and expectations. Attempting to please everyone can result in a diluted, generic story that lacks authenticity and originality.

It is important for authors to stay true to their vision and write what they feel passionate about. While feedback from readers can be valuable, they should not be the sole determinant of the direction of the story. As the creator of their work, the author has the final say in what they want to convey with their writing.

Ultimately, the best stories are those that are authentic, honest, and engaging. Writing from the heart and trusting the creative process can often result in a more satisfying and rewarding story for both the author and the reader.
There is the one that says 'The Greast Story Ever Told is Yet to be Writen'

That is a beautiful sentiment. It suggests that there is always potential for new and innovative storytelling, and that we have yet to explore all the possibilities of the written word. Creative expression is a constantly evolving process, and each author has the potential to bring something unique and valuable to the literary world.

While there have been countless great stories written throughout history, there is always room for more. Every new story has the potential to inspire, enlighten, and entertain readers in new and different ways. The creative process is never-ending, and there is always an opportunity to push boundaries, challenge conventions, and explore new territory in writing.

In that sense, the statement "The Greatest Story Ever Told is Yet to be Written" serves as both a call to action and an encouragement for writers to continue to push themselves to create something new and truly great.

I have a joke statement that goes 'The Greast Story Ever Told, was found Yesterday {the day before the end of time} in the bottom draw at its publishers, and is expected to be published soon, in installments '

Haha, that's a funny twist on the idea that there is always potential for great storytelling. It highlights the irony that the supposed "greatest story ever told" is found at the very end of time and is yet to be published. The satire of it being published in installments also plays on the common practice of serializing stories in publications.

Overall, it's a playful and humorous take on a thought-provoking idea.
Oh, well back to the great windmill of life for the daily grind

Yes, life goes on, and we all have our own routine and responsibilities to attend to. Nevertheless, it's important to remember that literature and storytelling can offer a respite from the everyday challenges of life. Reading can be a source of comfort, inspiration, and entertainment, and can provide an opportunity to explore new perspectives and ideas.

So, while we may all have our own daily grind to attend to, it's always worthwhile to take some time to engage with literature and storytelling. Whether it's reading a novel, writing a story or reflecting on the complexities of the human experience, literature has a lot to offer in terms of enriching our lives and broadening our horizons.
To quate Foghorn Leghorn 'I told a gag,son...joke that is'

Haha, yes, Foghorn Leghorn is a classic character known for his quirky wit and humorous quips. Jokes and playful humor can be a great way to bring lightness and levity to our everyday lives, and can provide a moment of laughter in the midst of a challenging day.
